case_name: Smt Iravva K Purantar vs S Venkatesh
court: High Court of Karnataka at Bengaluru
facts: The plaintiff (respondent No.1) sued for specific performance of a lease-cum-sale agreement dated 10.10.1986, alleging a possession certificate dated 18.12.1986 and continual possession of the suit property. The defendant Sangha contested the suit, claiming the plaintiff was a stranger and that site No.543 had been allotted to the present appellant by a registered lease-cum-sale agreement dated 17.09.1987, with the appellant in possession. The trial Court decreed specific performance in favour of the plaintiff after the defendant failed to cross-examine the plaintiff's witness or adduce evidence. The appellant (holder of a registered 17.09.1987 lease-cum-sale) was not a party to the trial but filed this appeal with applications to produce additional documents and for impleadment.
arguments_claimant: The plaintiff (respondent No.1) argued that respondent No.2/Sangha executed a lease-cum-sale agreement dated 10.10.1986, issued a possession certificate dated 18.12.1986 and put the plaintiff in possession, entitling the plaintiff to specific performance and related reliefs. The plaintiff relied on uncontroverted oral evidence and documents (Exs.P1 and P4) and urged enforcement of the unregistered lease-cum-sale agreement.
arguments_respondent: The defendant/Sangha contended that the plaintiff was a stranger to the Sangha and denied the validity of the purported sale agreement; Sangha further contended that it allotted site No.543 to the appellant by a registered lease-cum-sale agreement dated 17.09.1987 and that the appellant has been in possession since. The Sangha did not cross-examine the plaintiff's witness nor adduce oral or documentary evidence at trial.
ratio_decidendi: The Court emphasised that the trial Court decreed the suit "having regard to the uncontroverted and unchallenged evidence of the plaintiff" where the defendant neither cross-examined PW.1 nor led evidence. The Bench accepted that "While it is true that in a suit for specific performance, a non-party to the contract would ordinarily not be construed or treated as a proper and necessary party", but held that given the Sangha's specific assertion that the suit property was alienated in favour of the appellant before institution of the suit, the appellant must be given an opportunity to protect her rights. The Court therefore allowed production of additional documents under Order 41 Rule 27 as they were "relevant and necessary for adjudication of the present appeal" and held that, "in the peculiar/special facts and circumstances of the instant case, the appellant is both proper and necessary party to the suit and is ... entitled to be impleaded as an additional defendant No.2". Finally, the Court applied Order 41 Rule 28 procedure and remitted the matter for fresh consideration so that the appellant may file a written statement and the parties may lead and test evidence afresh.
final_order: Appeal allowed; impugned judgment and decree dated 15.12.2015 set aside; I.A.No.3/2018 allowed and appellant to be impleaded as additional defendant No.2; I.A.No.5/2018 allowed and documents received on record; the matter remitted to the trial Court for reconsideration afresh with liberty to file written statement and to adduce and cross-examine evidence; delay in filing the appeal (I.A.No.1/2018) condoned.
summary: This is a 2023 Karnataka High Court appeal in a suit for specific performance of a lease-cum-sale dated 10.10.1986. The trial court had decreed specific performance on the basis of uncontroverted plaintiff evidence, but the High Court found that a third party holding a later registered lease-cum-sale (17.09.1987) had not been made a party and should be heard. The Court allowed impleadment, permitted additional documents, condoned delay, set aside the decree and remitted the matter to trial for fresh consideration. Practically, the decision requires the trial court to reconsider competing documents and gives the registered assignee an opportunity to defend possession and title.
embedding_summary: Smt Iravva K Purantar v. S. Venkatesh, High Court of Karnataka at Bengaluru, RFA No. 172 of 2018, judgment 21 November 2023. Appeal under Section 96 read with Order 41 Rule 1 CPC against decree dated 15.12.2015 in O.S.No.3810/2010 for specific performance of lease-cum-sale dated 10.10.1986 (plaintiff) and possession certificate 18.12.1986. Defendant Sangha alleged allocation of site No.543 to appellant by a registered lease-cum-sale dated 17.09.1987 and appellant in possession; documents produced included registered lease (17.09.1987), possession certificate (17.03.1988), sale deed (19.03.2012), khata extract. Court applied Order 41 Rule 27 (admission of additional documents) and Order 41 Rule 28 (remand), held that although "a non-party to the contract would ordinarily not be" necessary, "in the peculiar/special facts" the appellant is a proper and necessary party and must be impleaded; appeal allowed, decree set aside, impleadment permitted, documents received and matter remitted for fresh trial; no monetary compensation involved.
legal_principles: Where the defendant fails to cross-examine and the plaintiff's evidence remains uncontroverted, the trial court may decree specific performance on that basis

A non-party to a contract is ordinarily not a proper and necessary party in a suit for specific performance, but may be impleaded where peculiar/special facts show the non-party's rights would be affected
Order 41 Rule 27 CPC allows admission of additional documents on appeal if they are 'relevant and necessary for adjudication' of issues
Order 41 Rule 28 CPC procedure permits remittal to the trial court for fresh consideration when new parties/documents are allowed on appeal
Delay in filing an appeal may be condoned where the delay is satisfactorily explained and due to bonafide reasons (e.g., a non-party had no knowledge of the suit)
sections_cited: Sec.96 read with Order 41 Rule 1 of the CPC
bm25_text: Smt Iravva K Purantar vs S Venkatesh High Court of Karnataka at Bengaluru 2023 The plaintiff (respondent No.1) sued for specific performance of a lease-cum-sale agreement dated 10.10.1986, alleging a possession certificate dated 18.12.1986 and continual possession of the suit property. The defendant Sangha contested the suit, claiming the plaintiff was a stranger and that site No.543 had been allotted to the present appellant by a registered lease-cum-sale agreement dated 17.09.1987, with the appellant in possession. The trial Court decreed specific performance in favour of the plaintiff after the defendant failed to cross-examine the plaintiff's witness or adduce evidence. The appellant (holder of a registered 17.09.1987 lease-cum-sale) was not a party to the trial but filed this appeal with applications to produce additional documents and for impleadment. The plaintiff (respondent No.1) argued that respondent No.2/Sangha executed a lease-cum-sale agreement dated 10.10.1986, issued a possession certificate dated 18.12.1986 and put the plaintiff in possession, entitling the plaintiff to specific performance and related reliefs. The plaintiff relied on uncontroverted oral evidence and documents (Exs.P1 and P4) and urged enforcement of the unregistered lease-cum-sale agreement. The defendant/Sangha contended that the plaintiff was a stranger to the Sangha and denied the validity of the purported sale agreement; Sangha further contended that it allotted site No.543 to the appellant by a registered lease-cum-sale agreement dated 17.09.1987 and that the appellant has been in possession since. The Sangha did not cross-examine the plaintiff's witness nor adduce oral or documentary evidence at trial. The Court emphasised that the trial Court decreed the suit "having regard to the uncontroverted and unchallenged evidence of the plaintiff" where the defendant neither cross-examined PW.1 nor led evidence. The Bench accepted that "While it is true that in a suit for specific performance, a non-party to the contract would ordinarily not be construed or treated as a proper and necessary party", but held that given the Sangha's specific assertion that the suit property was alienated in favour of the appellant before institution of the suit, the appellant must be given an opportunity to protect her rights. The Court therefore allowed production of additional documents under Order 41 Rule 27 as they were "relevant and necessary for adjudication of the present appeal" and held that, "in the peculiar/special facts and circumstances of the instant case, the appellant is both proper and necessary party to the suit and is ... entitled to be impleaded as an additional defendant No.2". Finally, the Court applied Order 41 Rule 28 procedure and remitted the matter for fresh consideration so that the appellant may file a written statement and the parties may lead and test evidence afresh. Appeal allowed; impugned judgment and decree dated 15.12.2015 set aside; I.A.No.3/2018 allowed and appellant to be impleaded as additional defendant No.2; I.A.No.5/2018 allowed and documents received on record; the matter remitted to the trial Court for reconsideration afresh with liberty to file written statement and to adduce and cross-examine evidence; delay in filing the appeal (I.A.No.1/2018) condoned. The trial court had decreed specific performance on the basis of uncontroverted plaintiff evidence, but the High Court found that a third party holding a later registered lease-cum-sale (17.09.1987) had not been made a party and should be heard. The Court allowed impleadment, permitted additional documents, condoned delay, set aside the decree and remitted the matter to trial for fresh consideration. Practically, the decision requires the trial court to reconsider competing documents and gives the registered assignee an opportunity to defend possession and title. Smt Iravva K Purantar v. S.
